input.paper_content: A prospective cohort study of 12,000 adults aged 40-65 followed over 8 years. Examined the association between daily step count (measured by accelerometer) and all-cause mortality. Participants wore accelerometers for 7 days at baseline. Results: compared to the lowest quartile (<4,000 steps/day), participants in the highest quartile (>10,000 steps/day) had a 40% lower risk of all-cause mortality (HR 0.60, 95% CI 0.48-0.75). The association was dose-dependent and persisted after adjustment for BMI, smoking, alcohol, and pre-existing conditions. Limitations stated: observational design, single measurement point, potential reverse causation (sicker people walk less).

A survey of 500 office workers found that 62% reported musculoskeletal pain. Those using standing desks for more than 2 hours/day reported 23% less lower back pain (p=0.02). Cross-sectional design. Self-reported outcomes.

A quasi-experimental study evaluating the impact of Germany's Energiewende (energy transition) on household electricity prices from 2010-2023. Used difference-in-differences methodology comparing German prices with a synthetic control group of comparable EU countries. Found that the renewable energy surcharge added an average of 6.2 cents per kWh to German household bills, a 28% increase over the study period. However, wholesale electricity prices in Germany fell by 15% over the same period due to the merit-order effect of zero-marginal-cost renewables entering the market. The net effect on industrial electricity prices was roughly neutral. Study funded by a government research grant. Limitations: synthetic control methodology sensitive to comparator selection; could not account for all tax and levy differences across countries.
input.story_angle: Whether renewable energy makes electricity more expensive for consumers
